question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2020

Code: 65/4/1 (set-1)

  1. Please check that this question paper contains 15 printed pages.
  2. Code number given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  3. Please check that this question paper contains 36 questions.
  4. Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  5. 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answerbook during this period.

MATHEMATICS

Time allowed:3 hours
Maximum Marks:80

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper comprises four Sections A, B, C and D. This question paper carries 36 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 20 comprises of 20 questions of 1 mark each.
  3. Section B - Questions no. 21 to 26 comprises of 6 questions of 2 marks each.
  4. Section C - Questions no. 27 to 32 comprises of 6 questions of 4 marks each.
  5. Section D - Questions no. 33 to 36 comprises of 4 questions of 6 marks each.
  6. There is no overall choice in the question paper. However, an internal choice has been provided in 3 questions of one mark, 2 questions of two marks, 2 questions of four marks and 2 questions of six marks. Only one of the choices in such questions have to be attempted.
  7. In addition to this, separate instructions are given with each section and question, wherever necessary.
  8. Use of calculators is not permitted.

Section – A

Question numbers 1 to 10 are multiple choice questions of 1 mark each.
Select the correct option:
1.

The value of sin-1{tex}\left(\cos \frac{3 \pi}{5}\right){/tex} is

a. {tex}\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{5}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{-\pi}{10}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{-3 \pi}{5}{/tex}
2.

If A = [2 -3 4], B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = [1 2 3] and Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex},  then AB + XY equals:

a. [28]
b. [24]
c. 28
d. 24
3.

If {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} 2 & 3 & 2 \\ x & x & x \\ 4 & 9 & 1 \end{array}\right|+3=0{/tex}, then the value of x is

a. 3
b. 0
c. -1
d. 1
4.

{tex}\int\limits_{0}^\frac{\pi}{8} {/tex} tan2 (2x) is equal to

a. {tex}\frac{4-\pi}{8}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{4+\pi}{8}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{4-\pi}{4} {/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{4-\pi}{2} {/tex}
5.

If {tex}\vec {a} \cdot \vec {b}=\frac{1}{2}|{\vec{a}}||{\vec{b}}|{/tex}, then the angle between {tex}\vec a{/tex} and {tex}\vec b{/tex}is

a. 0o
b. 30o
c. 60o
d. 90o
6.

The two lines x = ay + b, z = cy + d; and x = {tex}a^{\prime}y + b^{\prime}, z = c^{\prime}y + d^{\prime}{/tex} are  perpendicular to each other, if:

a. {tex}\frac{a}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{c}{c^{\prime}}=1{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{a}{a^{\prime}}+\frac{c}{c^{\prime}}=-1{/tex}
c. aa' + cc' = 1
d. {tex}aa^{\prime} + cc^{\prime} = –1{/tex}
7.

The two planes x - 2y + 4z = 10 and 18x + 17y + kz = 50 are perpendicular, if k is equal to:

a. -4
b. 4
c. 2
d. -2
8.

In an LPP, if the objective function z = ax + by has the same maximum value on two corner points of the feasible region, then the number of points at which zmax occurs is:

a. 0
b. 2
c. finite
d. infinite
9.

From the set {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, two numbers a and b (a {tex}\neq{/tex} b) are chosen at random. The probability that {tex}\frac{a}{b}{/tex} is an integer is:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{3}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{3}{5}{/tex}
10.

A bag contains 3 white, 4 black and 2 red balls. If 2 balls are drawn at random (without replacement), then the probability that both the balls are white is:

a. {tex}\frac{1}{18}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{1}{36}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{1}{12}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{1}{24}{/tex}

In Q. Nos. 11 to 15, fill in the blanks with correct word/sentence:

11.

If f : R {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} R be given by f(x) = {tex}\left(3-x^{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}{/tex}, then fof (x) = ________.
12.

If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+\mathrm{y} & 7 \\ 9 & x-\mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & 7 \\ 9 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then x{tex}\cdot{/tex}y = ________
13.

The number of points of discontinuity of f defined by f(x) = |x| – |x + 1| is ________.
14.

The slope of the tangent to the curve y = x3 - x at the point (2, 6) is ________.

OR

The rate of change of the area of a circle with respect to its radius r, when r = 3 cm, is ________.
15.

If {tex}\vec a {/tex} is non-zero vector, then {tex}(\vec a. \hat i)\hat i{/tex} + {tex}(\vec a. \hat j)\hat j{/tex} + {tex}(\vec a. \hat k)\hat k{/tex} equals ________.

OR

The projection of the vector {tex}\hat{\text i}-\hat{\text j}{/tex} on the vector {tex}\hat{\text i}+\hat{\text j}{/tex} is ________.

Q. 16 to 20 are very short answer questions.

16.

Find adj A, if A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 & -1 \\ 4 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.
17.

Find {tex}\int \frac{2^{x+1}-5^{x-1}}{10^x} d x {/tex}
18.

Evaluate {tex}\int\limits_0^{2 \pi}{/tex} |sin x| dx
19.

If {tex}\int\limits_0^{\mathrm{\text a}} \frac{\mathrm{d} \text x}{1+4 \text x^2}=\frac{\pi}{8}{/tex}, then find the value of a.

OR

Find {tex}\int \frac{\mathrm{d} \text x}{\sqrt{\text x}+\text x}{/tex}
20.

Show that the function y = ax + 2a2 is a solution of the differential equation {tex}2\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d}\text x}\right)^2+\text x\left(\frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} \text x}\right)-\mathrm{y}{/tex} = 0.

Section – B

Q. Nos. 21 to 26 carry 2 marks each.

21.

Check if the relation R on the set A = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6} defined as R = {(x, y): y is divisible by x} is (i) symmetric (ii) transitive

OR

Prove that {tex}\frac{{9\pi }}{8} - \frac{9}{4}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{1}{3} = \frac{9}{4}{\sin ^{ - 1}}\frac{{2\sqrt 2 }}{3}{/tex}
22.

Find the value of {tex}\frac{d y}{d x}{/tex} at {tex}\theta{/tex} {tex}=\frac{\pi}{3}{/tex}, x = cos {tex}\theta{/tex} - cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex}, y = sin {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2{tex}\theta{/tex}.
23.

Show that  f(x) = (x – 1) ex + 1 is an increasing function for all x > 0.
24.

Find |{tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} and {tex}|\vec{b}|{/tex}, if {tex}|\vec{a}|{/tex} = {tex}2|\vec{b}|{/tex} and {tex}(\vec{a}+\vec{b}) \cdot(\vec{a}-\vec{b}){/tex} = 12.

OR

Find the unit vector perpendicular to each of the vectors {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}=4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+\hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex} and {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}=2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-\hat{\mathrm{j}}+2 \hat{\mathrm{k}}{/tex}.
25.

Find the equation of the plane with intercept 3 on the y-axis and parallel to xz - plane.
26.

Find [P({tex}\frac BA{/tex}) + P({tex}\frac AB{/tex})] if P(A) {tex}=\frac{3}{10},{/tex} {tex}P(B)=\frac{2}{5}{/tex} and {tex}P(A \cup B)=\frac{3}{5}{/tex}.

Section – C

Q. Nos. 27 to 32 carry 4 marks each.

27.

Prove that the relation R on Z, defined by R {(x, y) : (x - y) is divisible by 5} is an equivalence relation.
28.

If y = sin-1 {tex}\left(\frac{\sqrt{1+\text x}+\sqrt{1-\text x}}{2}\right){/tex}, then show that {tex}\frac{\text {dy}}{\text {dx}}=\frac{-1}{2 \sqrt{1-\text x^2}}{/tex}

OR

Verify the Rolle’s Theorem for the function f(x) = ex cos x in {tex}\left[-\frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}
29.

Evaluate: {tex}\int\limits_0^\pi \frac{\text x \sin \text x}{1+\cos ^2 \text x} \mathrm{~d} \text x {/tex}.
30.

For the differential equation given below, find a particular solution] satisfying the given condition
{tex}(x+1) \frac{\mathrm{dy}}{\mathrm{d} x}=2 \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{y}}+1{/tex}, y = 0 when x = 0.
31.

A manufacturer has three machines I, II and III installed in his factory. Machine I and II are capable of being operated for atmost 12 hours whereas machine III must be operated for atleast 5 hours a day. He produces only two items M and N each requiring the use of all the three machines.
The number of hours required for producing 1 unit of M and N on three machines are given in the following table:

Items Number of hours required on machines
I II III
M 1 2 1
N 2 1 1.25

He makes a profit of ₹ 600 and ₹ 400 on one unit of items M and N respectively. How many units of each item should he produce so as to maximize his profit assuming that he can sell all the items that he produced. What will be the maximum profit?
32.

A coin is biased so that the head is three times as likely to occur as tail. If the coin is tossed twice, find the probability distribution of number of tails. Hence find the mean of the number of tails.

OR

Suppose that 5 men out of 100 and 25 women out of 1000 are good orators. Assuming that there are equal number of men and women, find the probability of choosing a good orator.

Section – D

Q. Nos. 33 to 36 carry 6 marks each.

33.

Using properties of determinates prove that: {tex}\left|\begin{array}{lll} \mathrm{a}-\mathrm{b} & \mathrm{b}+\mathrm{c} & \mathrm{a} \\ \mathrm{b}-\mathrm{c} & \mathrm{c}+\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{b} \\ \mathrm{c}-\mathrm{a} & \mathrm{a}+\mathrm{b} & \mathrm{c} \end{array}\right|{/tex} = a3 + b3 + c3 - 3 abc.

OR

If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3&2 \\ 2&0&{ - 1} \\ 1&2&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then show that A3 - 4A2 - 3A + 11I = O, Hence find A-1.
34.

Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = (x - 1)3 (x - 2)2 is

  1. strictly increasing
  2. strictly decreasing.

OR

Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.
35.

Find the area of the region lying in the first quadrant and enclosed by the x-axis, the line y = x and the circle x2 + y2 = 32.
36.

Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{a}{/tex} are coplanar and the plane containing them is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex}

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper comprises four Sections A, B, C and D. This question paper carries 36 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. Section A - Questions no. 1 to 20 comprises of 20 questions of 1 mark each.
  3. Section B - Questions no. 21 to 26 comprises of 6 questions of 2 marks each.
  4. Section C - Questions no. 27 to 32 comprises of 6 questions of 4 marks each.
  5. Section D - Questions no. 33 to 36 comprises of 4 questions of 6 marks each.

1.

The value of sin-1{tex}\left(\cos \frac{3 \pi}{5}\right){/tex} is

a. {tex}\frac{\pi}{10}{/tex}
b. {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{5}{/tex}
c. {tex}\frac{-\pi}{10}{/tex}
d. {tex}\frac{-3 \pi}{5}{/tex}
Correct Answer: (c) {tex}\frac{-\pi}{10}{/tex}
Explanation:

{tex} \sin ^{-1}\left(\cos \frac{3 \pi}{5}\right) {/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^{-1}\left(\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\frac{3 \pi}{5}\right)\right) {/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^{-1} \sin \left(\frac{5 \pi-6 \pi}{10}\right) {/tex}
{tex}= \sin ^{-1} \sin \left(-\frac{\pi}{10}\right) {/tex}
{tex}= -\frac{\pi}{10} \epsilon\left[-\frac{\pi}{2} , \frac{\pi}{2}\right]{/tex}

 1
2.

If A = [2 -3 4], B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, X = [1 2 3] and Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 2 \\ 3 \\ 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex},  then AB + XY equals:

a. [28]
b. [24]
c. 28
d. 24
Correct Answer: (a) [28]
Explanation:

AB = [2 -3 4], B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 3 \\ 2 \\ 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

    He makes a profit of ₹ 600 and ₹ 400 on one unit of items M and N respectively. How many units of each item should he produce so as to maximize his profit assuming that he can sell all the items that he produced. What will be the maximum profit?

    Solution:

    Suppose x and y be the number of items M and N respectively.
    Total profit on the production = {tex}₹ (600x + 400y){/tex}
    Maximize {tex}Z = 600x + 400y{/tex}
    Subject to constraints
    {tex}x + 2 y \leq 12{/tex} (Constraint on Machine I)
    {tex}2 x + y \leq 12{/tex} (Constraint on Machine II)
    {tex}x + \frac { 5 } { 4 } y \geq 5{/tex} (Constraint on Machine III)
    s.t. {tex}x \geq 0 , y \geq 0{/tex}
    Reducing the linear constraints into the linear equations.
    {tex}x + 2y = 12{/tex} ... (i)
    {tex}2x + y = 12{/tex} ... (ii)
    {tex}4x + 5y = 20{/tex} ... (iii)
    {tex}x = 0, y = 0{/tex} ... (iv)

    Equations Points of Intersection
    (i) and (ii) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x = 4, y = 4{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is (4, 4)

      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is {tex}\left( \frac { - 20 } { 3 } , \frac { - 28 } { 3 } \right){/tex}
    (i) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 6

    (ii) and (iii) x = 6 and {tex}y = \frac { - 4 } { 5 }{/tex}
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is {tex}\left( 6 , \frac { - 4 } { 5 } \right){/tex}
    (ii) and (iv) when x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 12
      {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Point is (0, 12)

    For {tex}x + 2 y \leq 12{/tex}, let x = 0, y = 0

    For {tex}2 x + y \leq 12{/tex} , let x = 0, y = 0

    A coin is biased so that the head is three times as likely to occur as tail. If the coin is tossed twice, find the probability distribution of number of tails. Hence find the mean of the number of tails.

    Solution:

    P(Head) {tex}=\frac{3}{4},{/tex} P(Tail) {tex}=\frac{3}{4},{/tex}
    Let X = number of tails. Clearly X can be 0, 1, 2 
    Probability distribution is given by

    X 0 1 2
    P(X) {tex}\frac{9}{16}{/tex} {tex}\frac{6}{16}{/tex} {tex}\frac{1}{16}{/tex}

    Mean {tex}=\sum X \cdot P(X)=\frac{6}{16}+\frac{2}{16}=\frac{1}{2}{/tex}

    Suppose that 5 men out of 100 and 25 women out of 1000 are good orators. Assuming that there are equal number of men and women, find the probability of choosing a good orator.

    Solution:

    Let A, E1 and E2 denote the events that the person is good orator, is man and is woman respectively.Therefote, we have,
    P(E1) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    P(E2) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    Now, we have,
    {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{1}})=\frac{5}{100}{/tex}
    {tex}P(\frac {A}{ E_{2}})=\frac{25}{1000}{/tex}
    Using Bayes' theorem, we get
    Required probability = {tex}P(\frac {E_{1}}{A}t)=\frac{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)}{P\left(E_{1}\right) P\left(A / E_{1}\right)+P\left(E_{2}\right) P\left(A / E_{2}\right)}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}}{\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{5}{100}+\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{25}{1000}}{/tex}
    {tex}=\frac{1}{1+\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{2}{3}{/tex}

    Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = (x - 1)3 (x - 2)2 is

    1. strictly increasing
    2. strictly decreasing.
    Solution:

    Given, {tex}f(x)=(x-1)^3(x-2)^2{/tex}
    On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get
    {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^3 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x-2)^2 {/tex}
    {tex} +(x-2)^2 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x-1)^3\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(u v)=u \frac{d v}{d x}+v \frac{d u}{d x}\right] {/tex}
    {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^3 \cdot 2(x-2) {/tex} {tex} +(x-2)^2 \cdot 3(x-1)^2 {/tex}
    {tex} =(x-1)^2(x-2)[2(x-1)+3(x-2)] {/tex}
    {tex} =(x-1)^2(x-2)(2 x-2+3 x-6) {/tex}
    or {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^2(x-2)(5 x-8){/tex}
    Now, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)={0}{/tex}
    or {tex}(x-1)^2(x-2)(5 x-8)=0{/tex}
    Either {tex}(x-1)^2=0{/tex} or {tex}x-2=0{/tex} or {tex}5 x-8=0{/tex}

    {tex} \therefore x=1, \frac{8}{5}, 2{/tex}
    Now, we find intervals and check in which interval f(x) is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing.

    Interval {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^2{/tex} {tex}(x-2)(5 x-8){/tex} Sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex}
    {tex}x<1{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(-){/tex} +ve
    {tex}1<x<\frac{8}{5}{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(-){/tex} +ve
    {tex}\frac{8}{5}<x<2{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(+){/tex} -ve
    {tex}x>2{/tex} {tex}(+)(+)(+){/tex} +ve

    We know that, a function {tex}f(x){/tex} is said to be an strictly increasing function, if {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} and strictly decreasing if {tex}f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} So, the given function {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing on the intervals {tex}(-\infty, 1)\left(1, \frac{8}{5}\right){/tex} and {tex}(2, \infty){/tex} and decreasing on {tex}\left(\frac{8}{5}, 2\right){/tex}.
    Since, {tex}f(x){/tex} is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at {tex}x=1, \frac{8}{5}, 2{/tex}. Hence, {tex}f(x){/tex} is

    1. increasing on intervals {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{8}{5}\right]{/tex} and {tex}[2, \infty){/tex}
    2. decreasing on interval {tex}\left[\frac{8}{5}, 2\right]{/tex}

    Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Also, find the maximum volume.

    Solution:

    Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively.

    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm
    {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i)
    Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y.
    Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} 
    {tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)]
    {tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} 
    On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get
    {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} 
    Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
    {tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
    {tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} 
    {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} 
    {tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} 
    Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get
    {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} 
    {tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} 
    At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum.
    So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)]
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder,
    {tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} 
    {tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} 
    {tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} 
    {tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex}

    Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{a}{/tex} are coplanar and the plane containing them is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex}

    Solution:

    Two lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_2+\mu \vec{b}_2{/tex} are coplanar if {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = 0
    Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex}
    = {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}\left(\because \vec{a}_1=\vec{a}, \vec{b}_1=\vec{b}, \vec{a}_2=\vec{b}, \vec{b}_2=\vec{a}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex}
    = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{b}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} \; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0
    Hence the lines are coplanar.
    Equation of plane containing them is given by,
    {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 [{tex}\because{/tex} {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{n}{/tex} = 0]
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left(\because\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]=0\right){/tex}

Find the intervals on which the function f(x) = (x - 1) 3 (x - 2) 2 is strictly increasing strictly decreasing. Solution: Given, {tex}f(x)=(x-1)^3(x-2)^2{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x , we get {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^3 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x-2)^2 {/tex} {tex} +(x-2)^2 \cdot \frac{d}{d x}(x-1)^3\left[\because \frac{d}{d x}(u v)=u \frac{d v}{d x}+v \frac{d u}{d x}\right] {/tex} {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^3 \cdot 2(x-2) {/tex} {tex} +(x-2)^2 \cdot 3(x-1)^2 {/tex} {tex} =(x-1)^2(x-2)[2(x-1)+3(x-2)] {/tex} {tex} =(x-1)^2(x-2)(2 x-2+3 x-6) {/tex} or {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^2(x-2)(5 x-8){/tex} Now, put {tex}f^{\prime}(x)={0}{/tex} or {tex}(x-1)^2(x-2)(5 x-8)=0{/tex} Either {tex}(x-1)^2=0{/tex} or {tex}x-2=0{/tex} or {tex}5 x-8=0{/tex} {tex} \therefore x=1, \frac{8}{5}, 2{/tex} Now, we find intervals and check in which interval f(x) is strictly increasing and strictly decreasing. Interval {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=(x-1)^2{/tex} {tex}(x-2)(5 x-8){/tex} Sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} {tex}x<1{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(-){/tex} +ve {tex}1<x<\frac{8}{5}{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(-){/tex} +ve {tex}\frac{8}{5}<x<2{/tex} {tex}(+)(-)(+){/tex} -ve {tex}x>2{/tex} {tex}(+)(+)(+){/tex} +ve We know that, a function {tex}f(x){/tex} is said to be an strictly increasing function, if {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} and strictly decreasing if {tex}f^{\prime}(x)<0{/tex} So, the given function {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing on the intervals {tex}(-\infty, 1)\left(1, \frac{8}{5}\right){/tex} and {tex}(2, \infty){/tex} and decreasing on {tex}\left(\frac{8}{5}, 2\right){/tex} . Since, {tex}f(x){/tex} is a polynomial function, so it is continuous at {tex}x=1, \frac{8}{5}, 2{/tex} . Hence, {tex}f(x){/tex} is increasing on intervals {tex}\left(-\infty, \frac{8}{5}\right]{/tex} and {tex}[2, \infty){/tex} decreasing on interval {tex}\left[\frac{8}{5}, 2\right]{/tex} 6 OR Find the dimensions of the rectangle of perimeter 36 cm which will sweep out a volume as large as possible, when revolved about one of its sides. Solution: Let breadth and length of the rectangle be x and y, respectively. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle = 36 cm {tex} \Rightarrow 2x + 2y = 36{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x + y = 18{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = 18 - x{/tex} ...(i) Let the rectangle is being revolved about its length y. Then, volume (V) of resultant cylinder {tex} = \pi {x^2}.y{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow V = \pi {x^2}.\left( {18 - x} \right){/tex} {tex}\left[ {\because V = \pi {r^2}h} \right]{/tex} [Using Eq. (i)] {tex} = 18\pi {r^2} - \pi {x^3} = \pi \left[ {18{x^2} - {x^3}} \right]{/tex} On differentiating both sides w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = \pi \left( {36x - 3{x^2}} \right){/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dV}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow 36x = 3{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3{x^2} - 36x = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3\left( {{x^2} - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 3(x)\left( {x - 12x} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow x = 0,12{/tex} {tex}\therefore x = 12\,\,\left[ {\because ,x \ne 0} \right]{/tex} Again, differentiating w.r.t. x, we get {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6x} \right){/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow {\left( {\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \left( {36 - 6 \times 12} \right) = - 36\pi < 0{/tex} At x = 12, volume of the resultant cylinder is the maximum. So, the dimensions of rectangle are 12 cm and 6 cm, respectively. [using Eq. (i)] {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum volume of resultant cylinder, {tex}{\left( V \right)_{x = 12}} = \pi \,\left[ {18.{{\left( {12} \right)}^2} - {{\left( {12} \right)}^3}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \,\left[ {{{12}^2}\left( {18 - 12} \right)} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \pi \times 144 \times 6{/tex} {tex}= 864\pi c{m^3}{/tex} 6 35. Show that the lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}+\lambda \vec{b}{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{b}+\mu \vec{a}{/tex} are coplanar and the plane containing them is given by {tex}\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}} \cdot(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}})=0{/tex} Solution: Two lines {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_1+\lambda \vec{b}_1{/tex} and {tex}\vec{r}=\vec{a}_2+\mu \vec{b}_2{/tex} are coplanar if {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = 0 Now, {tex}\left(\vec{a}_2-\vec{a}_1\right) \cdot\left(\vec{b}_1 \times \vec{b}_2\right){/tex} = {tex}(\vec{b}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} {tex}\left(\because \vec{a}_1=\vec{a}, \vec{b}_1=\vec{b}, \vec{a}_2=\vec{b}, \vec{b}_2=\vec{a}\right){/tex} = {tex}\vec{b} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{b}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a} \; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 Hence the lines are coplanar. Equation of plane containing them is given by, {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 [ {tex}\because{/tex} {tex}(\vec{r}-\vec{a}) \cdot \vec{n}{/tex} = 0] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a})-\vec{a} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\vec{r} \cdot(\vec{b} \times \vec{a}){/tex} = 0 {tex}\left(\because\left[\begin{array}{lll} \vec{a}\; \vec{b} \; \vec{a} \end{array}\right]=0\right){/tex} 6
